title: Polyprotic acids and staged ionization
definition: A **polyprotic acid** has more than one ionizable proton and gives them up in **stages**, each a separate equilibrium with its own constant: $\mathrm{H_3PO_4} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{H_2PO_4^-}$ ($K_{a1}$), then $\mathrm{H_2PO_4^-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{HPO_4^{2-}}$ ($K_{a2}$), then $\mathrm{HPO_4^{2-}} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{PO_4^{3-}}$ ($K_{a3}$). Successive constants fall steeply — typically $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2} \gg K_{a3}$, each roughly $10^5$ smaller — because pulling a proton off an already-negative ion is far harder. That separation is what makes the arithmetic simple: the **first** ionization sets $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ almost entirely, so the pH is essentially the first stage's, and each later stage is the same ICE solve run on the concentrations the stage before it left. A useful consequence for a diprotic-or-more acid: the **amphiprotic middle** species sits at a concentration $\approx K_{a2}$, because after stage 1 $[\mathrm{H^+}] \approx [\mathrm{H_2PO_4^-}]$ and the second-stage mass-action law collapses to $K_{a2} \approx [\mathrm{HPO_4^{2-}}]$.
